coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive detection of commercial vitamin B9 and B12 by graphene nanobuds through inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ravi, Pavithra V.
Thangadurai, T. Daniel
Nataraj, D.
coredata.dc:description: Folic acid (FA or vitamin B9) and Methyl Cobalamin (MeCob or vitamin B12) are vital controllers of homocysteine metabolism. Increased intake of FA and vitamin B12 plays a crucial role in the prevention of cardiovascular disease and the reduction of mortality by lowering serum homocysteine levels. Therefore, fluorescent probes for monitoring FA and MeCob level alternation are crucial and greatly preferred. Reported herein is a new fluorescent probe, 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (DAN) functionalized graphene quantum dots (DAN-GQD), which sensitively respond to FA and MeCob at a very low acceptable concentration in H2O. The sensitive detection behavior of DAN‒GQD towards FA and MeCob was examined by absorption and fluorescence techniques. The addition of FA (100 × 10−12 M) and MeCob (25 × 10-9 M) gradually increases the absorption intensity of DAN-GQD (5 × 10‒9 M) owing to static interaction and dynamic equilibrium, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of aqueous solution of DAN‒GQD (5 × 10‒9 M) shows ca. 2-fold quenching upon the addition of FA (ФF 0.54 → 0.31) and MeCob (ФF 0.54 → 0.30), which is mainly due to inner filter effect (IFE). Job's plot analysis divulges that DAN‒GQD bind with FA in 1:2 and with MeCob in 1:1 stoichiometry. DAN‒GQD showed an excellent sensitivity towards FA and MeCob with a limit of detection (LoD; (S/N = 3)) of 1.73 × 10‒15 M and 6.37 × 10‒12 M, respectively. The time-resolved fluorescence decay profile confirms that quenching of DAN–GQD upon the addition of FA and MeCob follows non-radiative relaxation of excited electrons. The morphology change, interference studies, the effect of time on FA and MeCob detection of DAN‒GQD, and the stability of DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complexes were also investigated.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folic acid (FA or vitamin B9) and Methyl Cobalamin (MeCob or vitamin B12) are vital controllers of homocysteine metabolism. Increased intake of FA and vitamin B12 plays a crucial role in the prevention of cardiovascular disease and the reduction of mortality by lowering serum homocysteine levels. Therefore, fluorescent probes for monitoring FA and MeCob level alternation are crucial and greatly preferred. Reported herein is a new fluorescent probe, 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (DAN) functionalized graphene quantum dots (DAN-GQD), which sensitively respond to FA and MeCob at a very low acceptable concentration in H2O. The sensitive detection behavior of DAN‒GQD towards FA and MeCob was examined by absorption and fluorescence techniques. The addition of FA (100 × 10−12 M) and MeCob (25 × 10-9 M) gradually increases the absorption intensity of DAN-GQD (5 × 10‒9 M) owing to static interaction and dynamic equilibrium, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of aqueous solution of DAN‒GQD (5 × 10‒9 M) shows ca. 2-fold quenching upon the addition of FA (ФF 0.54 → 0.31) and MeCob (ФF 0.54 → 0.30), which is mainly due to inner filter effect (IFE). Job's plot analysis divulges that DAN‒GQD bind with FA in 1:2 and with MeCob in 1:1 stoichiometry. DAN‒GQD showed an excellent sensitivity towards FA and MeCob with a limit of detection (LoD; (S/N = 3)) of 1.73 × 10‒15 M and 6.37 × 10‒12 M, respectively. The time-resolved fluorescence decay profile confirms that quenching of DAN–GQD upon the addition of FA and MeCob follows non-radiative relaxation of excited electrons. The morphology change, interference studies, the effect of time on FA and MeCob detection of DAN‒GQD, and the stability of DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complexes were also investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamins are very essential for the human body and pondered as important nutrients obligatory in specific amounts. They cannot be generated in an adequate amount by the human body; so, they must be gained from the food [1]. In total, thirteen types of vitamins are known and each one has a distinctive leading role in human body metabolism [2]. Vitamins are classified based on their biological and chemical activities; for example, either water-soluble or fat-soluble. Among the thirteen vitamins, nine are water-soluble (eight B vitamins and vitamin C) and the remaining four are fat-soluble (vitamin A, D, E, and K) [3]. Except vitamin B12, these water-soluble vitamins are defecated from the body quickly and are not stowed for a long time. In contrast, fat-soluble vitamins are more likely to be stowed in the body due to accumulation in the intestine in the presence of lipid, which leads to hypervitaminosis more than the water-soluble vitamins [3]. Nevertheless, both vitamin B9 (Folic Acid (FA)) and vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) (Fig. 1 ) are very essential for body cell growth and development [4].

FA, a synthetic form of vitamin B9, acts as a cofactor in numerous significant biological reactions, for example, synthesis, repair, and methylation of DNA [5]. Folate, a natural form of vitamin B9 in food, is very much required for (i) cell division, (ii) infancy and pregnancy, (iii) functioning of the nervous system, and (iv) prevention of anemia by generating healthy red blood cells [6,7]. In addition to that, folates play a major role in the cerebral metabolism of cobalamin, acetylcholine, L-tyrosine, and methionine [8]. Likewise, vitamin B12 is very important for several biological functions such as (i) supplying essential methyl groups for protein and DNA synthesis, (ii) extraction of energy from proteins and fats, (iii) production of hemoglobin to carry oxygen in red blood cells, and (iv) digestion [6,9]. Both FA and MeCob are considered to be very important since they are interrelated by a mechanism called methyl trap hypothesis and operate very close together in the production of red blood cells, controlling the blood levels of the amino acid homocysteine and assists iron function properly in the body [10]. The decreased concentration of folate levels in the tissues is a physiologically functional deficiency caused by the methyl trap [11].

The standard level of FA and MeCob in the human body is 7.2–17.2 ng/mL and 200–900 pg/mL, respectively [12,13]. Deficiency of folic acid leads to many health effects, such as cardiovascular problems, eliminating toxins inside the body [14], symptoms of depression, concentration difficulties [15], fat and fatty acid metabolism, impaired function of RNA and DNA, muscle formation [16,17], megaloblastic anemia and birth defects [18]. MeCob deficiency also leads to many health problems and issues, such as body weakness or paralysis, loss of sensation, intelligence, learning, and impairment of memory, learning disabilities, gastrointestinal disorders, obesity, depression, urinary and bowel dysfunction, tendonitis, and allergies [19]. Besides, MeCob deficiency can also be involved in (i) megaloblastic anemia and (ii) neural tube defects, as related to folic acid [20] and (iii) the disturbance in the folate mechanism [11]. Therefore, to facilitate sound health and a lengthy lifespan, developing a rapid, trustworthy, and cost-effective method for simultaneous real-time determination of these two important vitamins with very high sensitivity is extremely essential.

Already existing literature reports point out that copious materials and traditional methods have been utilized to determine FA individually [21–27] as well as simultaneously [28–32] along with other biomolecules. MeCob is mainly studied independently through different principles and mechanisms [33–37]. However, those traditional analytical techniques have many disadvantages; for instance, expensiveness and restricted portability. Recently, numerous fluorescent/colorimetric chemosensors based on nanostructured materials and/or organic molecules for FA and MeCob detection have been developed separately [22,26,38,39]. On the other hand, for simultaneous determination of analyte of interest, such as FA and MeCob, one has to carefully design a structurally modified material that could promote the highly selective and ultrasensitive detection of an analyte. The unique properties such as long-term resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness [40,41], and tunable surface functionalities making GQDs promising candidates for sensors, optoelectronic devices, bioimaging and so on [42,43]. Surface modification or functionalization is a supplementary method to improve the quantum yield and an imperative intermediate for the subsequent functionalization of GQDs [44,45]. Although there are a few reports available for the functionalization of GQDs with organic molecules/polymers for sensing applications, none of them have demonstrated FA and MeCob detection [46–49].

Because of the aforementioned aspects and extension of our studies on carbonaceous materials, [50] herein we report 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (DAN) functionalized GQDs (DAN–GQDs) for simple, fast, selective and ultra-sensitive detection of FA and MeCob in water with LoD as 1.73 × 10−15 M and 6.37 × 10‒12 M, respectively. Furthermore, morphology change, fluorescence decay profile, interference studies, the effect of the time, and the stability were also investigated and reported. In the current study, both commercial and analytical grade samples of FA and MeCob were utilized for all the spectrophotometric experiments and the results were compared with each other.

All the reagents and solvents involved in synthesis were analytical grade and used without any further purification. Citric Acid, 1,8-Diamino Naphthalene, and Folic Acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Methyl Cobalamin injection from Neurokind and commercial FA was purchased from a local pharmacy.

UV–vis absorption was carried out on the Analytik Jena spectrophotometer (Model SZ–100, Germany). The excitation slit width was 1 nm. Fluorescence experiments were recorded on Agilent Technologies spectrofluorophotometer (G9800A) at 298 K with the excitation and emission slit of 5 nm each. FESEM (SIGMA HV – Carl Zeiss with Bruker Quantax 200 – Z10 EDS Detector) at Coimbatore Institute of Technology, Coimbatore, India. Micro Raman spectral analysis was carried out using Horiba Lab Ram HR with 532 lasers, 1800 grating, at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. EDX analysis was carried out using FESEM-FEI Quanta 250 Bruker, at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. The PL lifetime measurements were carried out using an IBH time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system, and the decay profile was deconvoluted using IBH data station software V2.6 at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. Infrared spectra were recorded on a Bruker ATR-FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Alpha-T, Germany). Raman Spectra recorded on LabRAM HR Evaluation with 514 nm laser excitation at Jeju National University, South Korea. The Zeta potential analyses were performed on the Horiba particle size analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was recorded on Thermo Scientific (MULTILAB 2000 Base system with X-ray, Auger, and ISS) with Twin Anode Mg/Al (300/400 W) X-ray source at CSIR-CECRI, Karaikudi, India.

Through the pyrolysis of Citric Acid (CA), surfactant-less GQD was prepared by following the literature procedure [50]. DAN-GQDs synthesis as follows: An equimolar concentration of DAN was dissolved in methanol and slowly added to the as-prepared surfactant-less GQDs solution under stirring condition. A brown color precipitate was started to appear after a few minutes of stirring which confirmed the formation of DAN-GQD and the stirring was continued for the next 2 h at room temperature. After stirring time elapsed, the precipitate formed was centrifuged and washed with deionized water (3 × 10 mL) to remove excess/unreacted DAN and diluted the DAN-GQD solution as required. The resultant solid product was dried at 40 °C for 1 h.

A stock solution of MeCob (50 × 10−9 M) was prepared by taking 2 mL of commercially available MeCob and diluting to 100 mL with deionized water. A stock solution of FA (200 × 10‒12 M) was prepared through serial dilution by measuring 0.882 g of both analytical grade and commercial FA and mixed with 100 mL with deionized water. DAN-GQD stock solution (10 × 10−9 M) was made with deionized water. All the experimental solutions were prepared with deionized water (Resistivity: >10 megaΩ.cm; Conductance: ≤ 0.1 mS/m; TOC: < 50 ppb; ASTM type: II+) (Milli Q, Millipore, Barnstead, CA, USA).

The UV–vis absorption studies were carried out to examine the interaction between (i) DAN-GQD and FA, and (ii) DAN-GQD and MeCob in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Initially, a quartz cuvette was filled with 2 mL of DAN-GQD solution which was used for corresponding titration experiments. UV spectrum analysis was run separately for each successive addition of 5 μL of an aqueous solution of FA (100 × 10−12 M) and MeCob (25 × 10‒9 M) solutions. The apparent association constant (Kapp) was calculated using the Benesi-Hildebrand plot from the absorption studies.

Fluorescence intensities were measured using Quartz cells. The emission value for DAN-GQD (λem 423 nm) was obtained upon excitation at 328 nm. We carried out fluorescence titration experiments by measuring the changes in fluorescence emission of DAN-GQD (λem 423 nm; λex 328 nm) upon the successive addition of 5 μL of an aqueous solution of FA and MeCob solutions separately. For all the measurements, excitation was at 328 nm and emission slit width was 5 nm. The initial volume of DAN-GQD was 2 mL. The titration experiment was repeated at least thrice to check the consistency. Stern–Volmer and modified Stern–Volmer equations were used to calculate the quenching constant (kq) and binding constant (Ka), respectively. The association constant (Kass) was calculated by the Scatchard plot and the dissociation constant (Kd) was calculated by Hill plot analysis. Time-correlated Single Photon Counting technique was used to study the excited state fluorescence lifetime for DAN-GQD before and after the addition of FA and MeCob.

The fluorescent material, DAN-GQD, was synthesized in nanobuds morphology and characterized by standard physiochemical techniques [50] (Figure SI1−3). The numerous fluorescence quenching mechanisms that have been reported so far are based on diverse photophysical processes, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), electronic energy transfer (EET) [51–53] and inner filter effect (IFE) [54,55]. All these above mentioned photophysical processes do not require the establishment of any covalent linking between the fluorophore and the quencher, which makes the quenching mechanism the most simple and flexible one when compared to others [56–60]. To understand the plausible quenching mechanism in the current work, i.e. IFE process, we have recorded the absorption and emission spectrum of FA, MeCob, and DAN-GQD separately (Fig. 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DAN-GQDs interaction with FA and MeCob

ce:section.ce:para:

and it was found to be 1.14 × 10−2 and 4.47 × 10-3 M-1, respectively (Fig. 5 ).

To explore the binding interaction between DAN-GQD and analyte, we measured the fluorescence emission intensity changes of DAN-GQD (5 × 10−9 M) that occurred upon the successive addition of FA (100 × 10-12 M; 0-1000 μL) and MeCob (25 × 10−9 M; 0−1000 μL) separately in H2O (Figs. 6 and 7
). Upon increasing the concentration of FA, the fluorescence emission intensity of DAN-GQD at 423 nm steadily decreased with slight redshift (ca. 3 nm) (Fig. 6A) [63], which reveals the strong interaction between FA and DAN-GQD. On the other hand, the addition of MeCob to an aqueous solution of DAN-GQD resulted in the quenching of fluorescence emission intensity of DAN-GQD without any shift in the wavelength (Fig. 7A). The efficient quenching of fluorescence intensity of DAN-GQD by both FA and MeCob is attributed to the IFE along with static and dynamic quenching, respectively [26]. To prove this hypothesis, we have recorded the absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum of DAN-GQD (5 × 10‒9 M), FA (100 × 10‒12 M), and MeCob (25 × 10‒9 M), individually. As shown in Fig. 2, the overlapping of absorption and emission peaks of FA and MeCob with DAN-GQD spectrum causes the IFE, which resulted in ca. 2-fold quenching of original emission intensity of DAN-GQD upon addition of both these analytes ((ФF 0.54 → 0.31 for FA) and (ФF 0.54 → 0.30 for MeCob)).

To find the binding stoichiometry of DAN-GQD with FA and MeCob, the Job’s plot analysis was carried out. The fluorescence titration profile divulges that DAN-GQD interacts with FA in 1:2 and with MeCob in 1:1 binding ratios respectively, which was conﬁrmed by the obtained maximum mole fraction of 0.6 (for FA) and 0.5 (for MeCob) at 423 nm (Fig. 8 A,B). Additionally, the binding stoichiometry between DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob is in good accordance with the nonlinear curve ﬁtting (Figs. 6B and 7 B).

The quenching of fluorescence emission intensity of DAN-GQD upon the addition of FA and MeCob is explained by the Stern-Volmer equation (eq. 2).

Where “fa” is the fraction of accessible fluorescence. The obtained Ka values (2.69 × 10−3 L/mol for FA, and 2.19 × 10-1 L/mol for MeCob) implies that DAN-GQD showed an excellent binding affinity with FA and MeCob, respectively, through IFE.

The binding association constant (Kass) and the number of sites (n) for the interaction of FA and MeCob with DAN-GQD surface were evaluated by the Scatchard plot analysis (Eq. (4)) [66]. The association constant (Kass) was calculated from the intercept and the binding site “n” was determined as the slope of the plot log

The association constant (Kass) values obtained from the linear Scatchard plot are 3.21 × 10−2 and 6.54 × 10-2 M−1 with an “n” value of 2 and 1 revealed that DAN-GQD strongly binds with FA and MeCob in the ratios 1:2 and 1:1, respectively, which is in harmony with Job’s plot analysis.

Ever since there is a high chance for more than one biomolecule to bind, to identify the type of binding between DAN-GQD and FA, and DAN-GQD and MeCob is a cooperative or non-cooperative, and to calculate the binding parameters, Hill plot was employed (Eq. (5)) [67].

Where “θ” is the fraction of active sites on DAN-GQD occupied by the FA and MeCob, “nH” is Hill coefficient, [Analyte] is the concentration of the analyte (FA or MeCob), and “Kd” is dissociation constant. Based on the above experiments, the obtained Hill coefficient value revealed that there is a positive accommodating binding site on the DAN-GQD surface for FA (nH = 2) and MeCob (nH = 1) molecules during the interaction. The Scatchard plot (Fig. 11A) indicates that the first bounded FA molecule persuades the affinity for the second FA molecule, which resulted in 1:2 stoichiometry with high dissociation constant (Kd = 3.03 × 10−2 M−1) (Fig. 12 A). In contrast, DAN-GQD interacts with MeCob only in one possible way with the lesser dissociation constant (Kd = 3.18 × 10−12 M−1) (Fig. 12B). The Hill plot analysis results clearly explain the existence of cooperative type binding between DAN-GQD∙FA and non-cooperative type binding between DAN-GQD∙MeCob, which is also subordinated with Job’s plot analyses.

To investigate the fluorescence lifetime profile of DAN‒GQD (λex 328 nm) in the absence and presence of FA and MeCob, a time-correlated single-photon counting experiment was carried out (Fig. 13 ). The obtained fluorescence lifetime profile for free DAN‒GQD (5.0 × 10−9 M) is best fitted with triexponential function to yield an average lifetime of 6.62 ns (Table 2). Addition of 1.0 equivalent of FA and MeCob to DAN‒GQD significantly increased the average lifetime (8.13 and 6.92 ns, respectively), but not changed the functionality. The existence of triexponential decay confirms the presence of two discrete species, such as the non-interacting and interacting fraction of DAN‒GQD molecule, and an additional lifetime component. Also, the increased lifetime indicates that the excited electrons stay in the excited state for a while before they choose non-radiative decay pathways [68]. Upon further increasing the concentration of FA and MeCob (5 equivalent), a trifling increase in the average lifetime was observed (8.13 → 8.79 and 6.92 → 7.75 ns, respectively) (Table 2). This noteworthy increase in an average lifetime explicates the formation of a ground-state complex between DAN-GQD and FA and MeCob, consequences the quenching rate, i.e. take a little longer time to re-emit to the ground state [64]. Furthermore, the increase in the average lifetime of DAN-GQD upon interaction with MeCob may also result in the formation of hydrogen bonds elicited through photolysis [69].

It is important to study the interference of other biologically important analytes such as metal ions, amino acids, and biological molecules on the efficient binding of DAN-GQD with FA and MeCob. Therefore, we examined the interference effect of metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+), amino acids (Alanine, Glycine, Leucine, Lysine, and Valine) and biological molecules (Ascorbic acid, Glucose, Citric acid, Adenosine-n-phosphates (AMP, ADP, and ATP)) at the higher concentration on the DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complex solutions, separately, through fluorescence technique. Addition of FA (100 × 10−12 M) and MeCob (25 × 10‒9 M) decreases the fluorescence intensity of DAN-GQD (5 × 10‒9 M), whereas ca. 100-fold higher concentration (500 × 10‒9 M) of these metal ions, amino acids, and biological molecules does not significantly change the emission intensity of DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD•MeCob (Fig. 14 A,C). The observed a very small decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity of DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD•MeCob complex might be due to dilution of the corresponding aqueous solutions. This phenomenon divulges that none of the above-mentioned interferon’s affect the strong binding nature of DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD•MeCob complexes even at 100-fold higher concentration and are also not competitive with FA and MeCob.

Besides, to investigate the interference effect of MeCob on the DAN-GQD•FA complex and the effect of FA on the DAN-GQD∙MeCob complex, we evaluated the fluorescence emission intensity changes of DAN-GQD∙FA solution upon addition of MeCob and vice-versa. Based on the fluorescence emission intensity changes, we confirmed that both FA and MeCob strongly bind individually with DAN-GQD, and the binding of MeCob with DAN-GQD is not affected by the addition of FA as interferon and vice-versa (Figure SI6 and SI7). The emission intensity of DAN-GQD (5 × 10−9 M; 2 mL) at 423 nm is immediately quenched and attained its almost 100 % fluorescence intensity in less than a minute by the addition of both FA and MeCob and remained constant upon further addition suggesting that stable complex exists between DAN-GQD and both FA and MeCob due to IFE (Fig. 15 A,B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination on the stability of complexes
ce:section.ce:para: To corroborate the stability of DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complexes in water, we have examined the fluorescence intensity changes of DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complex solutions to time, separately, by using the fluorescence technique. The fluorescence emission intensity of DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complex solutions did not show any significant changes in the emission intensity value at 423 nm even after 6 h which confirms high stability of DAN-GQD∙FA and DAN-GQD∙MeCob complexes in water (Figure SI8).

Furthermore, the stability of DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD•MeCob complexes were investigated by zeta potential measurements. The zeta potential value of free DAN-GQD (5 × 10−9 M) was found to be ca. −66.0 mV, which is owing to the presence of negatively charged functional groups (−OH and −COOH) on the surface and it also suggests that DAN-GQD is highly stable nanomaterial [70,71]. Addition of 1.0 and 2.0 eq. of FA decreases the zeta potential value (−66.1 → −62.9 → −51.4 mV) and further addition (3.0 eq.) causes no significant change in the zeta potential value (−50.6 mV) (Figure SI9). Similarly, the zeta potential value of DAN-GQD was decreased upon the addition of 1.0 eq. of MeCob (−66.0 → −56.5 mV) and remains almost constant upon further increasing the concentration (2.0 and 3.0 eq.; −55.7 and −54.1 mV, respectively). This obtained almost constant zeta potential values of DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD•MeCob complexes after the addition of 3.0 eq. of FA and 2.0 eq. of MeCob, might plausibly be due to the attainment of 1:2 and 1:1 stoichiometry ratio, respectively.

Most of the nanoparticles are found to be excitation dependent may be due to various reasons such as size, quantum confinement, etc. [72–75]. We examined the excitation dependency of as-prepared DAN-GQD at a different wavelength (310 → 350 nm). Upon different excitations, the emission wavelength of DAN-GQD was not changed (λem 423 nm) aside from the intensity, which gradually increased up to the maximum excitation (λex 328 nm) and started to decrease further (Figure SI10) [76]. The property of excitation independence is once again unresolved that the as-prepared DAN-GQD contains no defect in its structure, which corresponds to the obtained Raman spectra of DAN-GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on morphology and structural defects of the probe

ce:section.ce:para: To examine the changes in the structure and surface morphology of DAN-GQD after sensing FA and MeCob, the resulting fluorescence titration experiment solutions (DAN-GQD•FA and DAN-GQD•MeCob, respectively) were subjected to FESEM analysis. The as-prepared DAN-GQD possesses nanobud morphology before the addition of FA and MeCob (Fig. 16 A and B) [50]. Fascinatingly, upon sensing of FA, the DAN-GQDwere positioned on FA, which has a flake structure [77], without any change in morphology (Fig. 16C and D). Congruently, the binding of MeCob also did not cause any change in the nanobud morphology of DAN-GQD (Fig. 16E and F). Furthermore, the EDAX (electron beam energy of 15 kV) analysis data confirm that percentage of nitrogen increased (2.59→14.13 and 12.69 %) when FA and MeCob added to DAN-GQD, respectively. In addition, the EDAX spectrum of DAN-GQD∙MeCob corroborates the presence of Co(II) along with carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen elements without any impurities in its composition (Figure SI11, SI12, and SI13).

Micro Raman spectroscopic studies were carried out to study the defects in the structure of DAN-GQD before and after the sensing of FA and MeCob. For the as-prepared DAN-GQD, strong D and G bands were observed at ca. 1344 and 1580 cm−1, respectively, which is a characteristic peak for graphene-based materials [78] (Fig. 17 ). The integrated intensity ratio between the D and G band (ID/IG) for pure DAN-GQD is ca. 0.741. However, upon complexation with FA and MeCob, there is no shift in the D and G band values instead of an increase in ID/IG ratio, i.e. ca .0.831 and 0.894, respectively, which is less than unity. This increased ID/IG ratio might be due to augmented structural defects in DAN-GQD upon interaction with FA and MeCob. Remarkably, though the DAN-GQD has experienced defects upon complexation with FA and MeCob, its morphology, nanobud, was not changed, which is confirmed by FESEM images. Furthermore, micro-Raman spectrum results provide additional confirmation that the prominent graphitic nature of DAN-GQD was not changed even after complexation with FA and MeCob.

In summary, a novel carbonaceous material, fluorophore functionalized graphene quantum dots with nanobuds morphology, is proficiently applied for the ultra-low sensitive detection of two of the most important biomolecule entities with different sizes and dimensions, vitamins B9 and B12, in the aqueous medium through spectrophotometric studies. The impetuous fluorescence quenching of DAN-GQD upon the addition of FA and MeCob is due to the inner filter effect during the complexation, which positively resulted in the very sensitive detection of FA and MeCob. The calculated association constant with “n” value revealed that DAN-GQD binds with FA and MeCob in 1:2 and 1:1 stoichiometric ratio, respectively, which is in accordance with Hill plot and Job’s plot analysis. The obtained limit of detection (1.73 × 10−15 M for FA and 6.37 × 10‒12 M for MeCob) is passably low for the detection of subpicomolar and subnanomolar concentration ranges of FA and MeCob, respectively, in many biological systems. The FESEM images recorded for DAN-GQD after sensing both FA and MeCob indicate that the nanobud morphology of DAN-GQD was not changed. It is noteworthy to mention that the graphitic nature of DAN-GQD was not changed even after complexation which was confirmed by Raman spectroscopy study results. More importantly, DAN-GQD as a sensor demonstrates adequate performance even with commercial FA (LoD 22.33 × 10−15 M; Figure SI14) and MeCob in H2O. Based on the literature survey and to the best of our knowledge, this is the first unswerving report on utilizing surface-functionalized GQD for the ultra-sensitive detection of vitamins B9 and B12 within very less than the acceptable concentration (Table 3 ). The current findings pave the way for the application of novel carbonaceous materials that could be used in biological analyte monitoring.
